Iii . rule concern a right order , and due proportion of diet , with some caution therein . to the intent that we may be happy in respect of eat , let we due order and use the same ; otherwise be abuse the same become evil , and will turn to our annoyance and misery . we ought therefore right to order and moderate our self in this matter , eat so much , of such kind , and in such sort , as may maintain our body and mind in good temper , and not any way distemper and annoy the same . but forasmuch as there be great difference in respect of the several kind of meat , of the climate , the time and manner of eat , and the age , complexion , and constitution of our body , and of our accustomary manner of live and exercise , this matter will require a very large discourse , but i shall only endeavour to give a taste concern some principal matter herein , yet so much as perchance will be necessary in this compendious discourse . first , brief then concern such as be of a phlegmatic complexion , of a dull , slow , lazy condition . it be best for they to use somewhat a spare diet ( especial at supper ) easy of digestion , dry , warm , free from vapour , not too fat and foggy , roast rather than sodden , bread white , and well bake , to eat a crust thereof , or some other hard and dry morsel at the end of their meal after drink . herb and root , hot and dry be best for they ; to name only some few and common , as marigold , Sperage , Persley , pennyroyal , rosemary , sage , marjoram , Baume , bettony , hyssop , time , Savory , Origan , Stychas , cowslip , young cress , Vervin , Setwell , germander , Angellico , the flower of meliot and camomile , Basill root , and the rest which be hot and dry in operation . also salt , salt meat , mustard and the like be sometime good for they , as likewise pepper , nutmeg , clove , Macis , ginger , cinnamon , and other spice . also concern such as be of a sanguine complexion , and of voluptuous , vain , and sensual condition , also for young man , a moderate diet , cool , soluble , and of little nourishment , be most convenient for they ; as also herb that be cold and dry in operation , such as be endive , succory , sorrel , borrage , buglosse , strawberry-leaves , cinqfoile , and other : also let they use fumitary , see , and such other as purge and cleanse the blood : as also open potion , julip and the like . now for such as be of a choleric complexion , of impatient , rash , or of quick , active , stir condition , a large quantity of diet may be allow to they then to the phlegmatic , so as their diet be not too salt , hot , hard , and dry , but rather it shall be cool , fresh , moist , and soluble ; and they may the rather eat such a more plentiful diet than other , because their hot and dry body be better , and soon able to digest the same ; and because such a liberal diet do allay the impatiency , and the extravagant lightness and wander of their spirit . and therefore solid meat be not amiss for they ; CHAP. ii of lie . in speak of this subject , i shall proceed in this method . 1. i shall show what a lie be , and wherein the formal nature of it consist . 2. i shall show the several sort , or kind of lie . 3. the great evil , and malignity of this sin . 4. Give some remedy , and direction against it . for the first . a lie be a voluntary utter of that which be against a man knowledge and conscience , with a intent to deceive . Mendacium est voluntaria falsi enunciatio fallendi causa . mentiri est contra mentem ire . in parabolici locutionibus sensus literalis est non qui per verba , sed qui per res , and facta designatur . G••son . Non adhibentur metaphorae , parabolae , apologi , aut aliae tropicae lo•uutiones intention fallendi , say docendi majore cum venustate , and voluptate . Damnanda est jesuitica aequivocatio and mentalis reservatio ; and hoc ipso mendatii convincuntur , quibus haec in usu sunt , nimirum , quia cum veram propositionem animo concipiant , falsum tamen enuntient . Davenant . in Colos. therefore he that speak what he think do not tell a lie , though he may speak a untruth , or that which be in it self false . and in such a case what he say , be falsiloquium , but not mendacium , a falsehood , but not a lie . he offend not against moral truth or veracity , because he speak as he think , and so he do not lie , but be himself mistake . it|be formal mendacium a formal and direct lie , when we express or affirm a thing otherwise than we conceive , or think , with a intent to deceive . 2. i come to consider the several sort or kind of lie . and so a lie be usual distinguish into Jocosum . Officiosum . Perniciosum . 1. Jocosum , when a man utter a lie in sport to make other merry . to this we may apply that of the prophet Hosea , Chap. 7. verse 3. they make the king glad with their wickedness , and the prince with their lie . they that tell lie mere to make other laugh , be guilty of this kind of lie . 2. Officiosum , when a man tell a lie to help another out of some present danger , or inconvenience . God himself will not be serve with a lie , Job 13.7 . will you speak wicked for God ? will you talk deceitful for he ? we may not lie for God cause , or glory ; much less may we do it for any man benefit . Plato be no good casuist for Christians , who allow a lie either to save a citizen , or deceive a enemy . and the piae fraud allow among the papist be also much of this nature . 3. CHAP. v. of drunkenness . in treat of this subject , i shall proceed in this method . 1. i shall show what be to be esteem drunkenness . 2. what be the cause of it . 3. the heinousness of this sin . 4. shall answer the vain excuse that person addict to this vice do usual make for themselves , 5. shall give some remedy and direction against it . for the first . what be to be esteem drunkenness . drunkenness be a voluptuous excess in drink to the depravation of reason . drunkenness and gluttony be sin much of the same nature , only there be this difference . the understand usual be more hurt and the reason more disturb by excess in drink than by excess in meat . drunkenness do usual more brutify a man , and make he more like a beast , than excess in eat do , and so be a more scandalous sin . and therefore humane law have provide a penalty against drunkenness , but not against gluttony . now drunkenness in the large sense extend both to the affection and to the effect . he be a drunkard reputative and in the sight of God ; who will drink to excess if he have it , and will lie at the alehouse or tavern , and drink as other do , but can by reason of his want of money ; so that he be not restrain by his will , voluntas est homo , nihil aliud sumus nisi voluntate . August . but by his necessity . and further , you must observe that it|be not only the high degree of drunkenness that be to be call by that name , but low degree of it also that disturb and disorder the reason and understand of a man , may just so be call . there be several degree of drunkenness short of the high . they that drink beyond what nature or moderate refreshment require , and spend whole day , or a great part of they at tavern or alehouse at that vile exercise of drink , though they do not drink themselves drink and can bear it and carry it away better than other , yet they be very culpable in the sight of God . some man that can bear much drink without intoxication may be more guilty ( in the sight of God ) of excess than other , who by a small quantity upon a surprise be drink before they be aware . he that have by drink disturb his reason , and disable and hinder it from the performance of its proper work and office , be drunken in some degree ; but he that have quite disable it , be stark drink . i know it will here be say that a glass of wine or strong beer moderate take be many time very useful to give a man a assistant alacrity in his work and duty , and the service God require of he . no doubt a man may lawful add the refocillation , refreshment and alacrity to his spirit which make he more ready and cheerful in the duty of his general or particular call . but the excess be that which we here speak against , as that which be always to be avoid and so much of the first particular what drunkenness be . 2. we come now to inquire into the cause of drunkenness . and we shall find there be many cause of it . 1. a inordinate love unto and a eager appetite after drink , which some have bring themselves unto by their debosh and excess . these person have so much of the brute in they , and so little of the man , that their appetite be quite too hard for their reason . they will tell you ( possible ) that they know they shall abstain , but they can ; and why can they not , namely , for want of a resolve will . and thus go on in this wicked course , their immoderate appetite to drink draw they to drink till they be drink and that drunkenness cause a preternatural thirst , and that thirst a new drunkenness , and so add drunkenness to hi•st , Deut. 29.19 . they by degree enslave themselves to this vice . 2. love of good fellowship ( as they call it ) and merry company , where they drive away all thought that favour of sound reason , the fear of God , or care of their soul . they love such company where they may talk their pleasure over their cup , and may laugh and scoff at those that be serious religious and dare not do as they do but alas ! the end of such mirth what be it but heaviness and grief and vexation of spirit . poor wretch ! be this the merriment you so much covet and delight in ? keep it to your self . no wise or good man envy you or will be your partner in it upon any term . be i your enemy , i will not wish you so much harm and misery as you voluntary choose for your self . 3. slight thought of this sin , not consider that it|be one of the black list which exclude out of heaven , 1 Cor. 6.9 , 10. know you not that the unrighteous shall not inherit the kingdom of God ? be not deceive , neither fornicator nor idolater , nor adulterer , nor effeminate , nor abuser of themselves with mankind , nor thief , nor covetous , nor drunkard , nor reviler , nor extortioner shall inherit the kingdom of God . and yet notwithstanding this dreadful sentence denounce by God in his holy word against this sin , there be some so vile and miserable deprave , as to think it a piece of bravery to drink stout and a sign of a strong brain to be able to bear much drink , and a great conquest over another man to drink he down , not consider in the mean time that while they conquer other in this vile manner at their cup , they themselves be shameful conquer and overcome by the devil . 4. not consider nor understand the extreme hurt that this kind of excess do unto their body . they apprehend not how much it spoil the temper of their stomach , ruin their health , and ordinary shorten their life . 5. another great cause of drunkenness be idleness . they that be of a idle slothful temper , and love not to take pain in a honest call , usual seek out idle companion like themselves , and so ( that they may drive away the time as they call it ) they go a pot together . 6. another very frequent cause of drunkenness be that vile custom of drink health and constrain other to pledge they , which Answ. our saviour ask the woman whether any man have condemn her accord to the law make in that case ; whereby he intimate , that if the sentence of death have be lawful pass upon she , he will not have repeal it ; for he come not to violate the law but to fulfil it . but our saviour himself refuse to condemn she , because he come not into the world to execute the office of a earthly judge , but of a mediator who be to procure the pardon of our sin through his merit and intercession . he come not to condemn but to save , and to give his life a ransom for many . and therefore he will not execute the office of a magistrate in adjudging she to death , but of a minister in call she to repentance and amendment of her life . and so much by way of answer to the excuse that such as be addict to this sin do use to make for themselves . 3. i come now in the last place to give some direction and to prescribe some remedy against it . 1. frequent pour forth thy soul in fervent and earnest prayer unto the lord that he will please to keep thou from this sin and all tendency to it . when Paul pray so earnest to be deliver from the thorn in the flesh , the messenger of Satan , God answer , my grace be sufficient for thou , my strength shall be perfect in thy weakness , 2 Cor. 12.8 , 9 2. use fast and abstinence and beat down the body . this unclean devil go not out by any mean so soon as by fast and prayer . a weak body indeed must be careful support , but a wanton and unruly body must be careful subdue . CHAP. Vii . of discontent . in treat of this argument , i shall speak to these six particular . 1. i shall show what discontent be . 2. the great sinfulness of it . 3. the folly of it . 4. shall show what true Christian contentment be , and wherein the nature of it consist . 5. shall show the amiableness , and excellency of it . 6. shall give some direction and mean for the attain of it . for the first . discontent be a unquiet frame of heart under our present condition , and express it self in murmur and repine thereat . for common inward vex and repine , and outward complain and murmur go together . 2. the great sinfulness of it may appear to we in these particular . 1. it|be a quarrel the wisdom of God , and a secret accuse and tax his providence , non judicandum de providentia divina ante quintum actum . as if he do not wise order the lot and condition of his people . holy Job under his great affliction be far from this temper ; for though he suffer so deep , yet he charge not God with folly in that severe dispensation towards he . but say , the lord give and the lord take away , bless be the name of the lord , Job 1.21 , 22. 2. it|be a quarrel his fatherly care over we , as if he have not any love or affection to we or any regard of we . 3. it|be a quarrel his faithfulness , as if he will not perform the many gracious promise he have make for our good . 4. it|be a secret accuse of his justice , as if he deal hardly with we , and punish we more than our sin deserve . qui in poenis murmurat , ferientis justitiam accusat . 5. it|be a carriage very unworthy of the hope and expectation of a christian . he that have his peace make with God in Christ , and a title to a heavenly inheritance , shall endeavour from that consideration to calm and compose his soul under his great suffering . 6. it|be unanswerable to the experience we have have of god former gracious deal with we , and help of we out of strait and difficulty . 7. it|be a great gratification to Satan . we can gratify the devil more ( who be a malcontented spirit ) than to murmur against our creator . to be of a discontent unquiet spirit be to be like the devil . he be restless and unquiet , always in opposition to God , always fret at his dispensation . 8. it|be a betray and expose we to great temptation . the devil scarce ever have so great power over any as over those that be discontent . oh the direful thing he draw such person unto . my heart even ache when i think of they , and my hand be ready to tremble while i write of they . some of they he draw to make a formal covenant with himself . other to do such thing against themselves that even nature abhor . so that all that love themselves shall beg of God to keep they , and shall watch over themselves as to this particular sin . for let they assure themselves that nothing betray we soon into the devil hand than discon•ent . it be a stock he use to graft his temptation upon . the devil will desire no great advantage against a soul than to find it in such a temper . he be never more busy about any than such person , and usual make dangerous use of the unquietness and repine of their spirit . 9 it|be such a temper as exceed unfit we for holy duty and for the service which otherwise we may do for God . and so much of the sinfulness of discontent . 3. i come now in the three place to show the folly of it . 1. it|be vain and bootless . for as one of the ancient well say , the misery and evil we suffer , and at which we be so apt to be vex and trouble , be either such as we can remedy or such as we can remedy . if they be such as we can remedy , then what a folly be it to vex and fret at they , see it be impossible to help they . our lesson then be patience and submission . Levius fit patientia quicquid corrigere est nefas . but if the evil we suffer be such as be remediable , then let we not vex or fret at they , but let we use all care and diligence to help our self and to remedy they ; and this be good advice , though give by a heathen philosopher . 2. it take away the comfort of what we enjoy . if a man enjoy a thousand mercy ( in which he ought to rejoice , and for which he ought to be thankful ) yet if his mind be discontent they will all seem as nothing to he . so that no one thing bereave a man more of the comfort he may enjoy in his life than discontent . 3. it make our affliction worse : a discontent person be like a man in a fever , that by his toss and tumble more increase his heat ; or like a bird in the lime-twig which the more it flutter the more it entangle it self . 4. it provoke God oftentimes to send new and more affliction upon we . what do the Israelites get by mumure in the wilderness but a long stay in the wilderness . Children that sob and be stomachful after whip common get another another whip for their pain . 5. it make a man life very uneasy both to himself and his relation and to all that be about he . no body care to be near such person as be always murmur and complain . and so much of this folly of discontent . 4. i come now in the four place to show what christian contentment be , and wherein the nature of it consist . it|be a sweet quiet gracious frame of spirit free submit to God alwise and fatherly disposal of we in every condition . there be some thing that this gracious frame may consist with , and some thing that be opposite to it . ( 1. ) it may consist with a due sensibleness of God hand in afflict of we . it|be not a stoical apathy or insensibility . the apostle give we a excellent rule ( take out of the proverb of Solomon , Prov. 3.11 . ) how we shall carry our self under affliction , Heb. 12.5 . my son despise not thou the chasten of the lord , nor faint when thou be rebuke of he . some bold and secure sinner be apt to despise and disregard the correct hand of God ; the weak sort of Christians be apt to sink in spirit , and faint under their affliction . now the apostle advise we to steer between these two , namely , to be sensible of God hand when it be upon we , and yet not to faint or despond under it . ( 2. ) it may consist with a humble complaint to God or man . we may groan under our affliction but we ought not to grumble . ( 3. ) it may consist with seek out for help and ease in a lawful way . for that be our duty and God expect it from we . but now the thing opposite to it be . ( 1. ) inward fret , vex , and tumultuation of spirit , and rise of the heart against the almighty . ( 2. ) sink of spirit despond and despair of help . ( 3. ) outward murmur complain and repine . ( 4. ) sinful shift to help our self . and so much of the nature of true Christian contentment . 5. i come now to show the amiableness and excellency of this frame of spirit . 1. true christian contentment be not a single grace but a constellation , a cluster of many grace , particular of faith , patience , humility , and self-denial . as the perfume oil that be pour upon Aaron head be make up of several sweet ingredient ; so be this grace a compound of many other grace ; where this be , we may assure our self there be many grace exercise . 2. it|be a frame and temper high please to God . the apostle tell we , 1 Pet. 3.4 . that a meek and a quiet spirit be in the sight of God of great price . there be hardly a high commendation of any grace in the scripture . that must needs be very excellent which be by God himself so high esteem . 3. it|be a frame of spirit that give God his due . they that be endue with it do acknowledge God as their lord and owner . they acknowledge his plenary dominion over they . and it|be fit and comely that we who receive our life and be from God , shall own his sovereignty and dominion over we . 4. it|be a frame of spirit that will be great reward ; for the more content any man be the great be his obedience to God , and the great his obedience the great will be his reward . beside , even in this world a placid , quiet , sedate frame of heart make our life easy and delightful and so carry its reward along with it , whereas a turbulent discontent frame of spirit disorder and disquiet a man beyond all expression . and so much of the amiableness of this s•• . 6. i come now to give some direction for the attain of it . 1. mortify pride which be the seminary of many sin , especial of this of repine . compare your suffering and your sin together , and see whether your sin be not far more than your suffering . Consider therefore not so much what you feel as what you deserve . 2. Consider how many mercy and blessing you daily enjoy , and stir up your self to be exceed thankful for they . thankfulness and discontent can stand together . therefore when ever you find your self tempt to unquietness of mind , think of your mercy , and provoke and stir up your heart to be thankful to God for they ; and that will drive away your discontent . 3.
